concepts.title: Group Rotating Call Distribution
concepts.summary: A call distribution method that rings subsets of call queue members in sequence rather than all members simultaneously. Each group of a defined size is rung for a set duration; if unanswered, the call moves to the next group. The pattern repeats until all members are exhausted, then falls through to overflow settings.
key_points: Configurable group member size and ringing duration per group
Sequential group ringing: group 1, then group 2, etc., until all members tried
Calls overflow to voicemail, forwarding, or another queue if all groups fail
Distinct from simultaneous ringing where all members ring at once

concepts.title: Call Queue
concepts.summary: A Zoom Phone construct that manages incoming call routing to a group of members. Call queues support multiple distribution methods (including group rotating and simultaneous) and overflow behaviors for unanswered calls.
key_points: Accessible under Phone System Management > Call Queues
Configured via a Profile tab with Business Hours section
Members are added to the queue and calls are distributed per the chosen method

concepts.title: Named Rotation Groups via Multiple Call Queues
concepts.summary: An alternative to simple group rotating that chains two call queues together via overflow routing. The first queue rings designated primary members simultaneously; if unanswered, overflow routes the call to a second queue with secondary members. This enables named, tiered escalation groups rather than anonymous group-by-group rotation.
key_points: Primary queue uses simultaneous distribution; secondary queue also uses simultaneous
First queue's overflow routes to second queue
Second queue's overflow handles final escalation (e.g., voicemail)
Provides more control over who is rung and in what order than basic group rotating

concepts.title: Overflow Settings
concepts.summary: Configuration that defines what happens to calls that are not answered within a call queue. Common overflow destinations include voicemail, call forwarding to another number, or routing to a different call queue. Essential for ensuring no call is dropped.
key_points: Triggered when no member in the queue answers
In group rotating, triggered after all groups have been tried
Can route to voicemail, external forwarding, or another call queue

user_roles.title: Account Owner
user_roles.summary: Has full privileges to create, configure, and manage call queues, including setting up group rotating distribution and named rotation groups. Can access Phone System Management and configure all call queue settings.
key_points: Full access to Phone System Management > Call Queues
Can configure all distribution methods and overflow settings
Requires Zoom Workplace license with Phone or standalone Zoom Phone plan

user_roles.title: Admin
user_roles.summary: Administrators with phone system privileges can manage call queues and configure group rotating settings. Same license and setup prerequisites as account owners.
key_points: Access to Call Queues management interface
Can configure group rotating and overflow settings
Requires initial Zoom Phone setup completion

task_flows.title: Configure Group Rotating for a Call Queue
task_flows.summary: Admins enable group rotating distribution on an existing call queue by navigating to the queue's Profile tab, selecting Group Rotating from the Call Distribution dropdown, and setting the group size and ringing duration. Overflow behavior must also be configured before saving.
key_points: Navigate to Call Queues, click queue name, then Profile tab
Dropdown selection under Business Hours: change from default to Group Rotating
Two numeric inputs: Group member size and Ringing duration per group
Requires explicit Confirm action before Save
Overflow configuration is a recommended follow-up step before saving

task_flows.title: Configure Named Rotation Groups Using Multiple Call Queues
task_flows.summary: A two-phase setup where admins create two separate call queues linked by overflow routing. The primary queue rings a named set of members simultaneously; if unanswered, calls overflow to a secondary queue with a different set of members, which itself has its own overflow escalation.
key_points: Phase 1: Create primary queue, add members, set distribution to Simultaneous, configure overflow to second queue
Phase 2: Create secondary queue, add different members, configure its overflow to voicemail or forwarding
End result: named tier-1 group rings first, named tier-2 group rings second, final overflow handles the rest
Provides deliberate, predictable call routing across known member groups

constraints.title: License and Setup Prerequisites
constraints.summary: Call queue management requires either a Zoom Workplace license with Phone included or a standalone Zoom Phone calling plan. Additionally, the initial Zoom Phone setup must be completed before call queues can be created or managed.
key_points: Zoom Workplace with Phone, or standalone Zoom Phone calling plan required
Initial Zoom Phone setup must be completed first
Account owner or admin privileges required

constraints.title: Group Rotating Member Order
constraints.summary: In the basic group rotating method, there is no guarantee of which specific members are in each group. The system divides members into groups automatically. For precise control over who is rung first, admins must use the named rotation groups pattern with multiple call queues.
key_points: Basic group rotating assigns members to groups arbitrarily—no manual ordering
Named rotation groups solve this by chaining queues with explicit member lists
Design implication: clearly communicate this limitation in the UI to prevent confusion

ux_patterns.title: Tab-Based Queue Configuration
ux_patterns.summary: Call queue settings are organized into tabs (e.g., Profile tab). Within a tab, related settings are grouped into logical sections (e.g., Business Hours). This pattern organizes complex configuration into manageable, scannable groups.
key_points: Profile tab contains the Business Hours section with call distribution settings
Section headers visually group related controls
Incremental save pattern: Confirm individual changes within a section before saving the whole queue

ux_patterns.title: Dropdown Distribution Selector
ux_patterns.summary: The call distribution method is selected via a dropdown control. Changing from the default (e.g., Simultaneous) to Group Rotating reveals additional dependent controls: group member size and ringing duration. This progressive disclosure pattern keeps the UI clean until the relevant option is chosen.
key_points: Dropdown toggle between distribution modes
Progressive disclosure: numeric inputs appear only when Group Rotating is selected
Confirm button required to apply the distribution change within the section

ux_patterns.title: Overflow Routing as Queue-to-Queue Chaining
ux_patterns.summary: Overflow configuration acts as an explicit routing link between call queues, enabling a chain-of-responsibility pattern. Designers can treat this as a visual call-routing builder where queues are connected nodes in a flow graph.
key_points: Overflow settings allow selecting another call queue as the destination
Enables multi-tier escalation chains: queue 1 -> queue 2 -> voicemail
Low-code routing: admins compose complex call flows without scripting
